fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BAUMANN J:

Arising from Orders made and Reasons delivered in this matter on 14 November 2025 (see Coppens & Fischer (No 4) [2025] FedCFamC1F 810 (“the Reasons for Judgment”)), the wife, Ms Fischer, filed an Application for costs on 12 December 2025.
In her Application, the wife seeks an order, inter alia, that the husband pay costs fixed in the sum of $132,841 “or otherwise as agreed and failing agreement as assessed on a solicitor and client basis”, together with costs incidental to the costs Application.
In support of the Application, the wife relied on an affidavit filed 12 December 2025 and written submissions filed 23 December 2025.
In response, the Respondent husband, Mr Coppens, filed an affidavit on 9 January 2026 and written submissions – in summary opposing any order for costs.
Importantly, the costs application only relates to the property aspects of the matter. I have given consideration to the material relied upon by the parties.

PRINCIPLES

The principles applicable to costs applications under the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) are well settled and, although the general rule is that each party bear their own costs (s 117(i)), if circumstances exist to justify an order for costs (after considering factors set out in s 117(2A)), then the Court is empowered to make such order as to costs as it deems is just. I rely upon, as necessary, findings made in the Reasons for Judgment.

FACTORS FOR CONSIDERATION

Financial circumstances

The wife’s financial position was clear after the Orders were made, in that the wife owns the home where she resides with the children; manages a share portfolio but is not currently working as a professional. The husband’s financial position is unclear. He has interests in Country G and the United States of America (at least) but continues to dispute the wife’s assertions as to his probable financial position – and the Court’s findings in that regard. Neither party had the benefit of legal aid funding.

Conduct of the parties in the proceedings

The husband failed to make full and timely disclosure and, in respect of the significant issue of his gambling losses, the quantification of those losses (before Judge Myers) was only possible by accessing the records of organisations where the husband chose to gamble. At paragraph 11 of his written submissions, the husband “denies any deliberate or contumelious failure to provide disclosure”, a submission I reject. In any event, the husband says “he says already paid handsomely from the judgment in the proceedings for any default in this respect”. I accept that the husband’s gambling losses do not of themselves amount to “litigation misconduct”.

Whether any party has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings

Whilst the husband was successful in surviving a “threshold” argument knockout – after the proceedings were transferred to Division 1, the wife’s articulated claim and proposed orders in respect of the property alteration proceedings never deviated from seeking an order that the husband’s Application be dismissed – the effect of which was that each party retained what they then owned. The husband was “wholly unsuccessful” in getting any order that the wife pay him anything.

Offers for settlement

Before Judge Myers delivered his Reasons for Judgment on 24 November 2022, the wife on 9 February 2022 made an offer to the husband to accept $101,000 to resolve the property proceedings. The husband did not accept that offer.
After the proceedings were transferred to Division 1 on 5 December 2022, the wife on 13 April 2023 made a new offer to the husband to pay him $131,000. This offer was made after an unsuccessful conciliation conference. The husband did not accept the offer.
Considering the wife’s claim for costs only relate to the financial proceedings after transfer to Division 1, the husband should have accepted the offer made on 13 April 2023. Whilst the parenting proceedings would no doubt have continued, at least the costs incurred by the wife in the property proceedings would have been avoided.

CONCLUSION

The husband, at paragraphs 28 to 30 of his written submissions, says:
the wife has failed to demonstrate circumstances justifying a departure from the general rule in s.117(1) of the Act. She has the benefit of the entirety of the assets located in Australia together with a substantial portfolio of shares.
the Husband has been largely left to bank accounts in the country of [Country G] and as being his most substantial assets, and for which the evidence at trial was to the effect that he has no control over. He cannot access his money.
For the above reasons, the application for costs should be dismissed.

(As per the original)

I reject this submission by the husband. A more careful reading of the Reasons for Judgment reveals the Court was not satisfied the funds in Country G were inaccessible – or even how much are in the bank accounts and further what the value is of his real property in that country.
I am more than comfortable in finding that circumstances justify an order for costs in the wife’s favour.

QUANTUM OF COSTS

The husband (at paragraph 26) says that if, contrary to his submissions, the Court were minded to make any costs order, such costs should be limited and assessed in the ordinary way.
The wife’s claim for costs, in my view, bears careful consideration because:
(a) it only relates to the financial aspect of the proceedings after the matter was transferred to Division 1 (that is after the husband was successful in his threshold argument);

(b) the wife has incurred costs totalling $540,463.97 – but this included both the threshold hearing and parenting proceedings. Of this sum, the wife deposes she incurred costs, post transfer, of:

Solicitor
	
$118,509.08

Counsel
	
$147,173.75

Total
	
$265,682.83
(c) the claim for $132,841 is 50% of the total costs since transfer to Division 1.
These parties deserve finality, and I accept the submission of the wife that costs should be fixed. As the trial judge, my impression was that more time at the hearing (and the material required) for the parenting hearing was required. I think a fair division is 40% of the total costs of $265,682 – being $106,272.80 – could be attributed to the property/financial dispute.
In the circumstances, I will include a contribution by the husband to the costs of the wife in respect of the property and financial proceedings to be fixed in the sum of $106,000, payable within 60 days.
I will allow a further sum of $1,500.00 for the Application for costs – meaning in total the husband shall pay to the wife $107,500 within 60 days. That is the order which appears at the commencement of these Reasons.
